planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall or spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to full shade; nandina is extraordinarily adaptable and one of the easiest landscape shrubs to grow in the South. Note: the berries are toxic to birds and other wildlife — plant non-fruiting cultivars ('Gulf Stream', 'Firepower') to eliminate this concern in naturalistic settings.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to full shade; nandina (heavenly bamboo) is one of the most widely planted landscape shrubs in the American South, prized for its four-season ornamental interest: delicate, feathery, compound foliage that emerges pinkish-bronze in spring, matures to bright green in summer, and turns brilliant scarlet-red in fall and winter; clusters of white flowers in late spring; and masses of bright red berries that persist through winter. It is extraordinarily adaptable, tolerating deep shade under trees, drought, poor soil, and heat with minimal care. Important ecological note: Nandina domestica is classified as invasive in many southeastern states as birds spread the seeds into natural areas; furthermore, the berries are toxic to cedar waxwings and other birds that eat them in large quantities. Select non-invasive, non-fruiting cultivars (compact 'Gulf Stream', 'Firepower', 'Fire Power', or dwarf 'Obsession Nandina') for residential use and avoid planting near natural areas. Compact cultivars at 2–3 feet are particularly useful for low-maintenance groundcovers in shade.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; the blood-red color is most vivid in full sun but tolerates and looks excellent in partial shade. Critically important: remove any all-green shoots immediately and in their entirety — all-green reversion is the invasive form of Imperata and must never be allowed to persist.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Japanese blood grass 'Red Baron' is one of the most visually striking ornamental grasses available, producing upright, narrow blades that emerge green at the base and deepen to an intense, blood-crimson red toward the tips — the color intensifying through summer and fall until the entire blade glows a brilliant red that appears almost translucent when backlit by the sun. It is outstanding as an accent plant among silver or chartreuse foliage, at the edge of water features, or in mixed containers. An important safety note: the cultivar 'Red Baron' (sold as Japanese blood grass) is the non-invasive selection that must be grown from vegetatively propagated red material only. Any all-green reverted shoots that appear must be removed and destroyed immediately, as the green form (cogongrass, Imperata cylindrica) is one of the world's most destructive invasive grasses, classified as a federal noxious weed in the US. Do not allow 'Red Baron' to produce flowers or seeds. Do not plant in warm climates (zone 9+) where conditions favor reversion.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring after temperatures are consistently above 65°F in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Persian shield performs best in bright, filtered shade and may develop leaf scorch in direct afternoon sun in hot climates. It roots readily from stem cuttings and can be overwintered indoors in colder zones.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Persian shield is one of the most flamboyantly beautiful foliage plants available for shaded tropical gardens, with large, lance-shaped leaves of iridescent silver-purple that shimmer with metallic, jewel-like blue-purple-silver tones that change with the viewing angle and lighting conditions — an almost impossibly vivid foliage display unlike anything else in the garden. In zone 8–11 it thrives outdoors in the warm, humid conditions of the Gulf Coast, particularly in sheltered, brightly shaded spots where the metallic leaves glow dramatically. Pinch growing tips frequently to encourage dense, bushy growth — unpinched plants become tall and leggy. It is outstanding as a container plant that can be moved indoors as a houseplant in winter (where it performs beautifully in indirect light) in colder zones. Propagates effortlessly from 4-inch stem cuttings rooted in moist potting mix.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; coral fountain establishes quickly and roots readily from stem cuttings. It is outstanding cascading over walls, planted at the top of raised beds, or in hanging baskets where the arching stems can drape downward.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; coral fountain (firecracker plant) is a uniquely textural and continuously blooming tropical perennial with masses of thin, arching, rush-like green stems cascading in a weeping habit, studded nearly year-round with small, tubular, coral-red flowers that hummingbirds visit with enthusiasm. The stems have virtually no leaves — the photosynthesis occurs in the green stems themselves, giving the plant an unusual, airy, almost fern-like texture when not in bloom that is strikingly different from any other flowering plant. It is exceptionally drought, heat, and salt tolerant once established and performs at its best cascading over retaining walls, spilling from large containers, or planted in raised beds where the arching, weeping habit shows to best effect. In zone 9 it may suffer frost damage to stems but typically regrows from established roots. A yellow-flowered form is also available. One of the most reliable and continuously blooming hummingbird plants for Gulf Coast gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow seed or plant container-grown plants after the last frost in any well-drained soil in full sun; ruby grass grows rapidly in heat and begins blooming within 60–90 days of germination. In zone 9–10 it self-sows to overwinter; in colder zones it is grown as a summer annual.

directions: Plant in any well-drained soil in full sun; ruby grass (Melinis nerviglumis 'Savannah') is one of the most spectacular ornamental grasses available for summer color, producing fluffy, shimmering panicles of tiny spikelets in brilliant shades of ruby-red, rose-pink, and burgundy that catch the light and glow with exceptional luminosity — particularly beautiful in the late afternoon sun when the entire plant appears to be lit from within. The fine-textured, narrow, reddish-tinged leaves complement the flowers perfectly. It begins blooming in early summer and continues through frost, and the dried seed heads add winter interest. Unlike many ornamental grasses, it blooms all season rather than just in fall. As a tender tropical grass, it is grown as a perennial in zone 9–11 and as a summer annual in colder zones, where it self-sows freely in mild winters. Excellent in mixed borders, containers, and as a seasonal accent plant wherever summer color is needed.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings, slips, or container-grown plants after frost danger passes in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; ornamental sweet potato vine grows extremely rapidly in summer heat — expect 18–24 inches of new growth per week in warm conditions. Pinch tips to control spread or allow to trail freely as desired.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade — ornamental sweet potato vines (non-edible foliage cultivars of Ipomoea batatas) are among the most vigorous and colorful summer foliage plants available for containers, window boxes, and landscape use, producing rapidly trailing stems with dramatically colorful leaves in lime-chartreuse ('Margarita', 'Illusion Emerald Lace'), deep purple-black ('Blackie', 'Sweet Caroline Purple'), and bronze-red ('Tricolor', 'Sweet Caroline Bronze') — colors that provide extraordinary contrast with flowering summer annuals. Unlike the edible sweet potato, ornamental cultivars are selected entirely for foliage effect and produce minimal, non-edible tubers. They thrive in the heat and humidity of Gulf Coast summers and are virtually indestructible through the growing season. In zone 9 the underground tubers sometimes survive mild winters and regrow. Outstanding as a spiller in large containers and hanging baskets, as a fast groundcover on slopes, and as a vivid foliage complement to tropical mixed borders.

planting.note: Direct sow seed after the last frost in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Vietnamese balm self-sows readily in mild climates and returns year after year in zone 8–9. It grows quickly and reaches harvest size within 60 days of germination.

directions: Direct sow in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade after the last frost; Vietnamese balm (kinh gioi) is one of the most important and distinctive aromatic herbs in Vietnamese cuisine, with a complex flavor profile that combines lemon, mint, and slightly pungent herbal notes in a fresh, cooling combination that is essential in classic Vietnamese dishes. The leaves are used fresh as a raw herb garnish in pho, spring rolls, banh mi, and grilled meat dishes, added at the last moment so the volatile aromatics are preserved. It looks similar to lemon balm (Melissa officinalis) but has a completely different and more complex fragrance. In Vietnam it is used both as a culinary herb and medicinally for digestive and respiratory complaints. It grows rapidly in warm weather and is highly productive with regular harvesting from the growing tips, which also keeps the plant compact and delays flowering. Self-sows freely in warm climates; treat as an annual in zones 7 and colder.
sources: FAO Plant Production and Protection Division

planting.note: Direct sow seed or plant divisions in fall in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean, rocky or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; Texas betony is native to dry limestone slopes and rocky arroyos and requires excellent drainage — it declines quickly in heavy, wet clay soils. The plant self-sows to naturalize in appropriate, well-drained conditions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; Texas betony is a native wildflower of the limestone hills of Texas and the arid Southwest, producing tubular, brilliant scarlet flowers in whorls along the square stems of a spreading mint-family plant from late winter through frost — one of the longest-blooming native perennials in the Southern garden. The vivid red flowers are irresistible to hummingbirds and provide one of the best native hummingbird plants for xeric, alkaline-soil gardens. It is extremely drought and heat tolerant, thriving in the conditions that defeat most flowering perennials in the hot, dry Southwest. The felted, gray-green leaves provide attractive texture even between bloom cycles. Self-sows freely in appropriate, well-drained conditions to naturalize. Outstanding in native plant gardens, xeric landscapes, and rock gardens in zone 7–11.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring after temperatures are reliably above 50°F in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; white firespike is even more shade tolerant than red firespike and performs beautifully as an understory plant under large shade trees in humid Gulf Coast gardens.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; white firespike is a lesser-known but equally attractive relative of the scarlet-flowered firespike (Odontonema tubaeforme), producing tall, graceful spikes of pure white tubular flowers on dark, glossy-leaved plants in late summer and fall. It is an outstanding shade-garden perennial for zone 9–12, providing important late-season white flower color in deep shade conditions where most other white-flowering plants require more light. The white flowers are particularly effective at brightening dark corners of the garden and glow beautifully in evening light. Like red firespike, it grows most vigorously and productively in the bright shade of large trees or pergolas; it is somewhat more cold-sensitive than the red species and benefits from mulching in zone 9. Propagate from stem cuttings taken in summer. White firespike is less commonly available than red firespike but increasingly offered by specialty nurseries in the Gulf South.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 2–3 inches deep in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; pinecone ginger thrives in the warm, humid conditions of the Gulf Coast and produces its most spectacular inflorescences in the heat of summer and fall. In zone 9 it goes dormant in winter and regrows reliably in spring.

directions: Plant rhizomes in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; pinecone ginger (shampoo ginger, wild ginger) is a spectacular tropical ginger grown for its unusual inflorescences — tight, compact, cone-shaped flower heads that look exactly like green pinecones on thick stems, which mature from green to brilliant red as the season progresses. The small, yellow flowers emerge briefly from between the bracts. The ripe, red cones produce a fragrant, slightly slimy liquid when squeezed that has been used as a hair conditioner and shampoo in Pacific Island and Asian cultures for centuries — the liquid has a pleasant, gingery fragrance and actually conditions hair effectively. The rhizomes are edible with a slightly bitter, medicinal ginger flavor and are used in Southeast Asian cooking. In zone 9 the tall, bamboo-like stems die back completely in winter and regrow from rhizomes in spring, providing both edible, fragrant, and ornamental value throughout the warm season.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture
